title: Apple upside-down cake
subtitle: Caramelised apple slices over a spiced sponge
excerpt: Apple upside-down cake is a lightly spiced sponge baked over a layer of fresh apple slices caramelised in butter and brown sugar. Turned out warm, the apple top is sticky and amber-edged, slightly soft where it sat in the caramel and firmer at the edges. It suits a spoonful of crème fraîche on the side.
sourceNotes: Upside-down cakes in the British and American tradition are well established in public-domain cookery. The apple version follows the same method as the pineapple form, substituting fresh apples caramelised briefly in the tin. The technique is documented in British baking manuals throughout the twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best served warm on the day. Reheat in a low oven for 10 minutes.

content.text: Apple upside-down cake begins with a shallow caramel in the base of the tin, apples sliced and fanned into it, then a lightly spiced sponge batter spread on top. When the cake comes out of the oven and is inverted, the apples are the top layer, glossy with caramel, soft at the centre and with slightly firmer edges. A Bramley or Cox apple has enough sharpness to cut through the sweetness of the sponge.

items.prepNote: medium, firm; peeled, cored, and sliced 5 mm thick

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line the base of a 23 cm round tin with baking paper. Melt the butter for the base in a small saucepan over medium heat. Add the brown sugar and stir until dissolved into a thick caramel, about 2 minutes. Pour into the lined tin. Fan the apple slices over the caramel in overlapping rings, working from the outside inward.

Beat the softened butter and caster sugar until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time. Add the vanilla. Fold in the flour and cinnamon. Spoon the batter carefully over the apples and smooth the top, taking care not to disturb the apple layer.

Bake for 38 to 42 minutes until the sponge is deep gold, has pulled away from the sides, and a skewer through the sponge layer comes out clean. Rest in the tin for 5 minutes. Run a palette knife around the edge and invert onto a serving plate. Lift the tin away slowly. Serve warm.
